text: A diagram illustrates the port placement for robotic hepatectomy, showing the patient positioned supine with a bump under the right abdomen and in a ten to fifteen degrees reverse Trendelenburg position. Black dots represent robotic ports, while blue dots indicate assist ports. The laparoscopic CUSA is used through the assist port at the right subcostal midclavicular line.
The surgeon prepares for specimen extraction, typically performed through a fan and steel incision or by extending the umbilical port site.

The surgeon begins the hilar dissection with a cholecystectomy, dissecting, clipping, and dividing the cystic duct and artery. The hilar plate is lowered, and the cystic duct stump is encircled with an endo loop to retract the bile duct medially. This exposes the posterior lateral liver hilum, where the right hepatic artery is identified and circumferentially dissected. Before dividing the right hepatic artery, it is occluded, and ultrasound with Doppler is used to confirm the patency of the left hepatic artery.

During the robotic hepatectomy, the surgeon uses ultrasound to ensure safety and precision. The right hepatic artery is tied with a silk tie, clipped, and divided, revealing the underlying right portal vein. The right portal vein is circumferentially dissected, with branches to segment six and caudate branches isolated and divided. The portal vein is then tied, clipped with endoclips, and divided, completing the hilar dissection and extrahepatic inflow control.

The surgeon performs retrohepatic caval dissection, separating the retrohepatic inferior vena cava from the liver. Multiple short hepatic veins are sequentially dissected and controlled using hemlock clips or divided with a vessel sealer. A larger inferior right hepatic vein is tied, clipped, and divided, with dissection continuing until the base of the right hepatic vein is visible, to be divided later during parenchymal division.

The surgeon marks the planned parenchymal division plane on the liver. The middle hepatic vein, previously identified with ultrasound, is also marked to ensure its preservation during the division. Indocyanine green is administered to visually demarcate the liver segments following inflow control.

The left hepatic lobe appears bright due to indocyanine green uptake, while the right lobe remains dark. Parenchymal transection is performed using the laparoscopic CUSA and robotic vessel sealer, effectively minimizing blood loss. Intrahepatic vascular and biliary structures are dissected and controlled with the laparoscopic CUSA.

The surgeon divides the right hepatic duct intraparenchymally, ensuring distance from the hepatic duct bifurcation and avoiding impingement on the left hepatic duct. The right hilar plate with the hepatic duct is encircled and divided using a robotic stapler with a vascular staple load. The right hepatic vein is dissected intraparenchymally and stapled with a robotic stapler, completing the parenchymal division portion of the operation.

The specimen is placed in a large endobag and extracted through a fan and steel incision. The liver remnant is inspected, showing good vascularization. Hemostasis is verified, and the left hepatic artery and left portal vein inflow are confirmed using Doppler ultrasound.
